title: Token-Bucket Rate Limiter for LLM APIs (Redis-Backed)
tldr: Production token-bucket rate limiter for LLM APIs with Redis backing. Per-user, per-tenant, per-IP buckets. 429 + Retry-After.
description: Atomic Lua-based token bucket: distributed, fair, microsecond precision. Returns 429 with Retry-After when bucket is exhausted; supports per-user OR per-tier limits.
when_to_use: You serve an LLM API to many users and need fair, distributed rate limiting. Token bucket smooths bursts (vs leaky bucket) and scales horizontally via Redis. Best fit for chat apps, internal LLM gateways, and multi-tenant SaaS.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) single-node app - use slowapi/in-memory; (2) you need global rate limits in milliseconds - Redis adds 1-3ms; (3) you're already on Cloudflare/AWS WAF rate limits - just use those.
quick_start: 1) `pip install redis fastapi`  2) Run Redis  3) Drop the dependency on your endpoint.
full_code:
"""Redis-backed token-bucket rate limiter for FastAPI."""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, Depends
from redis.asyncio import Redis


# Atomic refill+consume in Lua so the whole operation is one round-trip.
LUA = """
local key = KEYS[1]
local capacity = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local refill_per_s = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local cost = tonumber(ARGV[3])
local now = tonumber(ARGV[4])

local data = redis.call('HMGET', key, 'tokens', 'ts')
local tokens = tonumber(data[1]) or capacity
local ts = tonumber(data[2]) or now

local delta = math.max(0, now - ts)
tokens = math.min(capacity, tokens + delta * refill_per_s)

local allowed = tokens >= cost
if allowed then tokens = tokens - cost end

redis.call('HMSET', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'ts', now)
redis.call('EXPIRE', key, 3600)

local retry_after = 0
if not allowed then
    retry_after = math.ceil((cost - tokens) / refill_per_s)
end
return {tonumber(tostring(allowed and 1 or 0)), tokens, retry_after}
"""


class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, redis: Redis, capacity: int = 60, refill_per_s: float = 1.0):
        self.redis = redis
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.refill_per_s = refill_per_s
        self._sha: str | None = None

    async def _load(self) -> None:
        if not self._sha:
            self._sha = await self.redis.script_load(LUA)

    async def consume(self, key: str, cost: int = 1) -> tuple[bool, int, int]:
        await self._load()
        result = await self.redis.evalsha(self._sha, 1, key, self.capacity, self.refill_per_s, cost, int(time.time()))
        allowed = bool(result[0])
        remaining = int(result[1])
        retry_after = int(result[2])
        return allowed, remaining, retry_after


# --- FastAPI integration ---
app = FastAPI()
redis = Redis.from_url(os.environ.get("REDIS_URL", "redis://localhost:6379"), decode_responses=True)
bucket = TokenBucket(redis, capacity=60, refill_per_s=1.0)


async def rate_limit(request: Request) -> None:
    user_id = request.headers.get("X-User-ID") or request.client.host
    allowed, remaining, retry_after = await bucket.consume(f"rl:user:{user_id}", cost=1)
    request.state.rl_remaining = remaining
    if not allowed:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=429, headers={"Retry-After": str(retry_after), "X-RateLimit-Remaining": "0"})


@app.post("/chat", dependencies=[Depends(rate_limit)])
async def chat(payload: dict, request: Request):
    return {"reply": "ok", "remaining": request.state.rl_remaining}
code:
import os, time, asyncio
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, Depends
from redis.asyncio import Redis

LUA = """
local key, capacity, refill, cost, now = KEYS[1], tonumber(ARGV[1]), tonumber(ARGV[2]), tonumber(ARGV[3]), tonumber(ARGV[4])
local data = redis.call('HMGET', key, 'tokens', 'ts')
local tokens = math.min(capacity, (tonumber(data[1]) or capacity) + math.max(0, now - (tonumber(data[2]) or now)) * refill)
local ok = tokens >= cost
if ok then tokens = tokens - cost end
redis.call('HMSET', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'ts', now); redis.call('EXPIRE', key, 3600)
return {ok and 1 or 0, tokens, ok and 0 or math.ceil((cost - tokens) / refill)}
"""

redis = Redis.from_url(os.environ.get('REDIS_URL', 'redis://localhost:6379'), decode_responses=True)
app = FastAPI()

@app.post('/chat')
async def chat(req: Request):
    user = req.headers.get('X-User-ID') or req.client.host
    res = await redis.eval(LUA, 1, f'rl:{user}', 60, 1.0, 1, int(time.time()))
    if not res[0]: raise HTTPException(429, headers={'Retry-After': str(res[2])})
    return {'ok': True}

production_checklist: Use Lua EVALSHA (script cached on Redis) - separate commands race under load.
Set bucket TTL (3600s) so abandoned users don't accumulate keys forever.
Return 429 with Retry-After + X-RateLimit-Remaining headers - clients use them.
Limit by user_id NOT IP for authed traffic - IP-limited shared-office traffic punishes innocent users.
Combine per-user + per-tenant + per-IP for layered defense.
Use cost-weighted buckets for expensive models - prevents Claude Opus abuse on a Haiku-pricing tier.
Run Redis with `maxmemory-policy=allkeys-lru` so the limiter never gets evicted under memory pressure.

faq.question: Token bucket vs leaky bucket vs fixed window?
faq.answer: Token bucket: smooths bursts + handles cost-weighted requests cleanly. Leaky bucket: enforces steady rate, kills bursty traffic. Fixed window: cheap but has the boundary-doubling problem. For LLM APIs where users send a burst then idle, token bucket is the right shape.

faq.question: Why Lua instead of Redis transactions?
faq.answer: EVAL is atomic + one round-trip. Pipelining HGET+compute+HSET is two round-trips and races under high concurrency. The 30-line Lua script is the difference between a working rate limiter and a leaky one.

title: Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter (Tenacity)
tldr: Tenacity decorator chain handling 429 + 5xx with jittered exponential backoff. Circuit-breaker fallback + per-provider rules.
description: Reusable retry decorator: tenacity primitives + provider-specific 429/Retry-After handling + a simple circuit breaker that opens on persistent failures.
when_to_use: Every LLM client should have this - providers occasionally 5xx, hit rate limits, or have transient network errors. Tenacity is the standard Python retry library; this recipe adds the LLM-specific Retry-After handling that most examples miss.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) your provider SDK already handles retries (Anthropic SDK does some); (2) you're writing a thin proxy that should just propagate errors; (3) requests are non-idempotent and retrying them is unsafe.
quick_start: 1) `pip install tenacity httpx`  2) Wrap your LLM call with @retry_llm  3) Watch retries happen automatically on 429/5xx.
setup_steps: Save the @retry_llm decorator + CircuitBreaker class in `retry.py`.
Decorate your LLM call functions with @retry_llm.
Call _breaker.check() at the top of each function and record_success/failure around the call.
Configure log level to WARNING to see retry-attempt logs.
Test the failure path: force a 503 from your provider and watch the retries happen.

import structlog
log = structlog.get_logger()
def log_retry(state):

common_errors.error_text: Retry-After header ignored, exponential backoff used instead
common_errors.cause: RateLimited exception not raised, just letting tenacity see HTTPStatusError.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Check status BEFORE raise_for_status() so you can read headers:
if r.status_code == 429:
    retry_after = float(r.headers.get('retry-after', '1'))
    raise RateLimited(retry_after_s=retry_after)
r.raise_for_status()

common_errors.error_text: Circuit breaker stuck open after provider recovers
common_errors.cause: fails counter never resets without a successful call.

production_checklist: Always honor Retry-After header for 429 - exponential backoff alone wastes the suggested wait time.
Cap stop_after_attempt at 5-7 - more retries on a real outage just delay the error to the user.
Log retry attempts to your observability stack with reason + wait time.
Use wait_exponential_jitter (NOT plain wait_exponential) - jitter prevents thundering herd.
Pair with a circuit breaker - retries on a fully-down provider are wasted compute.
Don't retry non-idempotent writes without an Idempotency-Key header.
Test the retry path in CI - it's the code most likely to break silently.

faq.question: Tenacity vs backoff library vs hand-rolled?
faq.answer: Tenacity: more features (retry conditions, before_sleep hooks, async support), slightly heavier API. backoff: simpler decorator but doesn't surface Retry-After cleanly. Hand-rolled: avoid - you'll forget jitter.

faq.question: When should retries open the circuit?
faq.answer: 5 consecutive failures in 60 seconds is a sane default. The actual right number depends on your traffic - on 10 QPS, that's 0.5s of full outage to trigger. Set thresholds via load testing, not vibes.

title: Server-Sent Events Streaming with FastAPI (Production)
tldr: FastAPI SSE endpoint streaming LLM tokens with proper Content-Type, heartbeat, and client-disconnect detection.
description: Production SSE: token streaming + 15s heartbeat to defeat proxies + client-disconnect handling + structured error events. Drop-in for any async LLM stream.
when_to_use: You're shipping a chat or completion API and want browser-native streaming. SSE is simpler than websockets, works through CDNs (with the right headers), and the EventSource API is built into every browser.
when_not_to_use: Skip SSE when (1) you need bidirectional comms - use websockets; (2) you're streaming binary - use websockets or chunked HTTP; (3) you're behind a proxy you can't configure (Cloudflare free tier buffers SSE).
quick_start: 1) `pip install fastapi uvicorn`  2) Save the endpoint  3) Connect from JS: `new EventSource('/chat?q=...')`.
full_code:
"""FastAPI SSE endpoint for streaming LLM tokens."""
from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import json
from typing import AsyncIterator

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic


app = FastAPI()
claude = AsyncAnthropic()


HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_S = 15  # CDNs/proxies kill idle connections at ~30-60s


async def _heartbeat() -> AsyncIterator[bytes]:
    """Emit a comment every N seconds to keep the connection alive."""
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_S)
        yield b": heartbeat\n\n"


async def _llm_stream(prompt: str) -> AsyncIterator[bytes]:
    """Stream tokens from Claude as SSE events."""
    try:
        async with claude.messages.stream(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=2000,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                # SSE event format: data: <payload>\n\n
                payload = json.dumps({"type": "delta", "text": text})
                yield f"data: {payload}\n\n".encode("utf-8")

            # Final usage event
            final = await stream.get_final_message()
            usage = {"input_tokens": final.usage.input_tokens, "output_tokens": final.usage.output_tokens}
            yield f"event: usage\ndata: {json.dumps(usage)}\n\n".encode("utf-8")
            yield b"event: done\ndata: [DONE]\n\n"
    except Exception as e:
        yield f"event: error\ndata: {json.dumps({'error': str(e)})}\n\n".encode("utf-8")


async def _merged_stream(prompt: str, request: Request) -> AsyncIterator[bytes]:
    """Interleave LLM output + heartbeats; abort if client disconnects."""
    llm = _llm_stream(prompt)
    hb = _heartbeat()

    llm_task = asyncio.create_task(_anext(llm))
    hb_task = asyncio.create_task(_anext(hb))

    while True:
        if await request.is_disconnected():
            llm_task.cancel(); hb_task.cancel()
            return

        done, pending = await asyncio.wait({llm_task, hb_task}, return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED, timeout=1.0)
        if not done:
            continue
        for t in done:
            try:
                chunk = t.result()
                yield chunk
            except StopAsyncIteration:
                # LLM finished
                for p in pending:
                    p.cancel()
                return
            if t is llm_task:
                llm_task = asyncio.create_task(_anext(llm))
            else:
                hb_task = asyncio.create_task(_anext(hb))


async def _anext(it):
    return await it.__anext__()


@app.get("/chat")
async def chat(q: str, request: Request):
    return StreamingResponse(
        _merged_stream(q, request),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache, no-transform",
            "Connection": "keep-alive",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",  # disable nginx buffering
        },
    )
code:
import asyncio, json
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

app = FastAPI(); claude = AsyncAnthropic()

async def stream(prompt, request):
    async with claude.messages.stream(model='claude-sonnet-4-5', max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}]) as s:
        async for text in s.text_stream:
            if await request.is_disconnected(): return
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'text': text})}\n\n".encode()
    yield b"event: done\ndata: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get('/chat')
async def chat(q: str, request: Request):
    return StreamingResponse(stream(q, request), media_type='text/event-stream',
        headers={'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', 'X-Accel-Buffering': 'no'})
setup_steps: Test from a browser: open the dev console + run `new EventSource('http://localhost:8000/chat?q=hi').onmessage = e => console.log(e.data)`.
For production behind nginx: add `proxy_buffering off; proxy_cache off;` to the location block.

variations.description: Needed for large prompts.

variations.label: Multi-event types (tool calls, citations)
variations.description: Emit different event: types for different content kinds.

variations.code_snippet: headers={'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', 'X-Accel-Buffering': 'no', 'CF-Cache-Status': 'BYPASS'}

common_errors.error_text: Browser shows nothing for ~60s then gets full response
common_errors.cause: Proxy is buffering SSE (nginx, Cloudflare).
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Headers: X-Accel-Buffering: no (nginx) AND proxy_buffering off in nginx config.
# Cloudflare: needs paid tier OR move to Workers.

common_errors.cause: No heartbeat - intermediate proxy/load balancer killed the idle connection.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Send heartbeats every 15s:
yield b': heartbeat\n\n'  # SSE comment - ignored by client but keeps conn alive

common_errors.error_text: EventSource not receiving 'event: done' as a custom event
common_errors.cause: Client side missed the addEventListener for that event type.

production_checklist: Always set X-Accel-Buffering: no header for nginx; configure proxy_buffering off in nginx.conf.
Send heartbeats every 15s - intermediate proxies kill idle connections.
Detect client disconnect via request.is_disconnected() - don't keep generating tokens for an abandoned client.
Use Cache-Control: no-cache, no-transform - some CDNs transform content otherwise.
Emit a final 'event: done' event - clients use it to close cleanly.
Wrap LLM stream in try/except + emit 'event: error' for client UX.
Test through your actual proxy chain in staging - SSE bugs only surface end-to-end.

faq.question: SSE vs websockets for LLM streaming?
faq.answer: SSE: simpler, works through HTTP infra, automatic reconnect in EventSource, one-way only. WebSocket: bidirectional, lower overhead per message at scale, but more proxy issues. For chat: SSE is the default unless you need user-to-server streaming mid-response.

faq.question: Why not just chunked transfer encoding?
faq.answer: SSE IS chunked HTTP with a defined message format (data:/event:/id:) and the EventSource API. Raw chunked transfer works but you'd rebuild the parsing on the client + lose automatic reconnect.

title: Redis LLM Response Cache (Exact + Semantic Two-Tier)
tldr: Two-tier cache: exact prompt-match in Redis (instant) + semantic-similarity fallback in vector index. 30-60% cost reduction on real workloads.
description: Production LLM cache: hash-based exact match for repeated prompts + embedding-based semantic match for near-duplicates. Wrapper around any LLM client.
when_to_use: Your traffic has repeat questions (FAQs, support, search). Even with prompt diversity, the top 20% of queries account for ~60% of volume. Exact + semantic cache cuts that to near-zero cost with sub-10ms latency.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) every request is unique (deeply personalized agents); (2) freshness is paramount (live data lookups); (3) responses are non-deterministic by design.
quick_start: 1) `pip install redis fastembed`  2) Run Redis  3) Wrap your LLM call with the @cached decorator.
full_code:
"""Two-tier cache: exact (Redis) + semantic (Redis with vector search)."""
from __future__ import annotations

import hashlib
import json
import os
import time
from typing import Callable, Any

import numpy as np
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
from fastembed import TextEmbedding


EMBED_MODEL = "BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5"   # 384-dim, fast
TTL_S = 60 * 60 * 24                      # 24h
SEMANTIC_THRESHOLD = 0.93                 # cosine similarity bar


class TwoTierCache:
    def __init__(self, redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379"):
        self.r = aioredis.from_url(redis_url, decode_responses=False)
        self.embedder = TextEmbedding(EMBED_MODEL)

    def _key_exact(self, prompt: str, system: str = "", model: str = "") -> str:
        h = hashlib.sha256(f"{system}|{model}|{prompt}".encode()).hexdigest()
        return f"llmcache:exact:{h}"

    def _key_index(self, model: str) -> str:
        return f"llmcache:semantic:{model}"

    async def get(self, prompt: str, system: str = "", model: str = "") -> str | None:
        # Tier 1: exact
        raw = await self.r.get(self._key_exact(prompt, system, model))
        if raw:
            return raw.decode("utf-8")

        # Tier 2: semantic
        q_vec = np.asarray(list(self.embedder.embed([prompt]))[0], dtype=np.float32)
        # Scan recent semantic entries (last 1000)
        entries = await self.r.zrevrange(self._key_index(model), 0, 1000, withscores=False)
        best: tuple[float, bytes] | None = None
        for member in entries:
            data = await self.r.hgetall(b"llmcache:sem:" + member)
            if not data:
                continue
            stored_vec = np.frombuffer(data[b"vec"], dtype=np.float32)
            sim = float(np.dot(q_vec, stored_vec) / (np.linalg.norm(q_vec) * np.linalg.norm(stored_vec)))
            if sim >= SEMANTIC_THRESHOLD and (best is None or sim > best[0]):
                best = (sim, data[b"answer"])
        return best[1].decode("utf-8") if best else None

    async def put(self, prompt: str, answer: str, system: str = "", model: str = "") -> None:
        # Exact tier
        await self.r.setex(self._key_exact(prompt, system, model), TTL_S, answer.encode("utf-8"))

        # Semantic tier
        q_vec = np.asarray(list(self.embedder.embed([prompt]))[0], dtype=np.float32)
        member = hashlib.sha256(f"{prompt}|{time.time()}".encode()).hexdigest()
        await self.r.hset(b"llmcache:sem:" + member.encode(), mapping={b"vec": q_vec.tobytes(), b"answer": answer.encode("utf-8"), b"prompt": prompt.encode("utf-8")})
        await self.r.zadd(self._key_index(model), {member.encode(): time.time()})
        # Cap the semantic index at 10k entries
        await self.r.zremrangebyrank(self._key_index(model), 0, -10001)


async def cached_call(cache: TwoTierCache, prompt: str, llm_fn: Callable[[str], Any], model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5") -> str:
    hit = await cache.get(prompt, model=model)
    if hit:
        return hit
    answer = await llm_fn(prompt)
    await cache.put(prompt, answer, model=model)
    return answer
code:
import hashlib, redis.asyncio as aioredis, json
from fastembed import TextEmbedding
import numpy as np

r = aioredis.from_url('redis://localhost:6379', decode_responses=False)
embed = TextEmbedding('BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5')

def key(prompt, model):
    return f'llmcache:{hashlib.sha256((model + prompt).encode()).hexdigest()}'

async def cached(prompt, llm_fn, model='claude-sonnet-4-5'):
    cached = await r.get(key(prompt, model))
    if cached: return cached.decode()
    answer = await llm_fn(prompt)
    await r.setex(key(prompt, model), 86400, answer)
    return answer
setup_steps: Wrap your LLM client function via `cached_call(cache, prompt, llm_fn=your_call_claude)`.
Watch the hit rate in production - typical 30-60% on chat / FAQ traffic.
Tune SEMANTIC_THRESHOLD higher (0.95) for sensitive content; lower (0.88) for general FAQ.
For >10k semantic entries, swap to Redis Stack RedisSearch with proper HNSW index.

common_errors.error_text: Hit rate is 0% despite repeated questions
common_errors.cause: Hashing includes timestamp or whitespace differs.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Normalize prompts before hashing:
import re
def _normalize(s): return re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', s.strip().lower())
h = hashlib.sha256(_normalize(prompt).encode()).hexdigest()

common_errors.error_text: Semantic cache returns near-matches that aren't actually right
common_errors.cause: Threshold too low.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
SEMANTIC_THRESHOLD = 0.95  # was 0.93 - tighter
# Better: a/b test thresholds on your traffic + measure quality drop

common_errors.cause: No TTL or no ZSET size cap.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Exact tier: SETEX with TTL
# Semantic tier: ZREMRANGEBYRANK to keep top 10k recent
await r.zremrangebyrank(self._key_index(model), 0, -10001)

production_checklist: Set TTL on every cache entry - never let it grow forever.
Cap the semantic index size + use ZREMRANGEBYRANK to evict oldest.
Namespace by user_id for any multi-tenant app - cross-user cache hits are data leaks.
Tune SEMANTIC_THRESHOLD on your traffic - 0.93 is a starting point, not gospel.
Track cache_hit_rate + cache_quality (sample 1% of hits and human-review).
For >10k entries move to Redis Stack with HNSW - linear scan stops at ~10k.
Don't cache responses with PII unless your Redis is on-prem + encrypted.

faq.question: Exact vs semantic - is semantic worth the complexity?
faq.answer: On real chat traffic, exact cache hits at 15-25%, semantic adds another 15-30% on top. For FAQ-shaped traffic the gap is larger; for code-gen traffic smaller. Run with exact-only first - if hit rate is satisfying, skip the semantic layer.

faq.question: Does this work for streaming responses?
faq.answer: Yes - cache the full completed response, replay it as a fast stream on hit. But you lose 'live' feel - some apps prefer always-fresh streams over cache.

title: Healthcheck + Readiness for LLM Pods (Kubernetes-Ready)
tldr: Health + readiness endpoints that probe model load, GPU memory, and downstream dependency latency. Drop-in for K8s + ECS.
description: Two-endpoint pattern: /healthz (cheap liveness) + /readyz (model loaded, deps reachable, GPU memory available). K8s probes use them for rolling deploys.
when_to_use: Any LLM service deployed on K8s, ECS, or similar orchestrators. The orchestrator needs to distinguish 'process is up' (liveness) from 'process is ready to serve traffic' (readiness) - especially when model loading takes minutes.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) running on a serverless platform that handles this for you; (2) you don't have downstream deps to probe (rare); (3) the model loads in <1s (still useful but lower value).
quick_start: 1) `pip install fastapi httpx`  2) Mount the /healthz + /readyz routes  3) Configure your Pod's livenessProbe + readinessProbe.
full_code:
"""K8s-ready /healthz + /readyz for LLM services."""
from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import os
import time
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response


STATE = {
    "model_loaded": False,
    "model_loaded_at": None,
    "last_dep_check_ok": True,
    "last_dep_check_at": 0.0,
}


async def _load_model() -> None:
    """Replace with your real model load."""
    print("[startup] loading model...")
    await asyncio.sleep(5)   # simulate
    STATE["model_loaded"] = True
    STATE["model_loaded_at"] = time.time()
    print("[startup] model ready")


async def _check_deps() -> bool:
    """Probe critical downstream dependencies."""
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=2.0) as c:
            r = await c.get(os.environ.get("VLLM_HEALTH_URL", "http://vllm:8000/health"))
            return r.status_code == 200
    except Exception:
        return False


async def _periodic_dep_check() -> None:
    while True:
        STATE["last_dep_check_ok"] = await _check_deps()
        STATE["last_dep_check_at"] = time.time()
        await asyncio.sleep(15)


@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    await _load_model()
    dep_task = asyncio.create_task(_periodic_dep_check())
    yield
    dep_task.cancel()


app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)


@app.get("/healthz")
async def healthz():
    """Liveness: cheap - 'is the process running?'"""
    return {"status": "ok"}


@app.get("/readyz")
async def readyz(response: Response):
    """Readiness: model loaded + deps reachable."""
    ready = STATE["model_loaded"] and STATE["last_dep_check_ok"]
    body = {
        "ready": ready,
        "model_loaded": STATE["model_loaded"],
        "model_loaded_at": STATE["model_loaded_at"],
        "last_dep_check_ok": STATE["last_dep_check_ok"],
        "last_dep_check_age_s": time.time() - STATE["last_dep_check_at"] if STATE["last_dep_check_at"] else None,
    }
    if not ready:
        response.status_code = 503
    return body


@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return {"app": "ossai-llm-service"}
setup_steps: Hit /healthz (always 200 once process is up) and /readyz (503 until model loaded).
In your Pod manifest: livenessProbe -> /healthz, readinessProbe -> /readyz.
Set startupProbe with longer timeout for slow model loads (avoid premature liveness failures).

variations.label: GPU memory check
variations.description: Fail readyz if VRAM is mostly consumed.

variations.label: K8s probe config
variations.description: Standard probe spec for your Pod.
variations.code_snippet:
containers:
- name: llm-service
  startupProbe:
    httpGet: {path: /readyz, port: 8080}
    failureThreshold: 30
    periodSeconds: 10
  livenessProbe:
    httpGet: {path: /healthz, port: 8080}
    periodSeconds: 30
  readinessProbe:
    httpGet: {path: /readyz, port: 8080}
    periodSeconds: 5

common_errors.cause: Liveness probe fires before model finishes loading.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Use startupProbe with high failureThreshold:
startupProbe:
  httpGet: {path: /readyz, port: 8080}
  failureThreshold: 60   # 10 min @ 10s interval
  periodSeconds: 10

common_errors.error_text: Readyz returns true but service fails on first request
common_errors.cause: Probe doesn't actually exercise the model.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Run a smoke inference in startup BEFORE setting model_loaded=True:
async def _load_model():
    model = load_real_model()
    _ = model.generate('hi', max_tokens=5)   # warmup + verify
    STATE['model_loaded'] = True

common_errors.error_text: Service marked unhealthy during a downstream blip
common_errors.cause: Dep check too aggressive - any single fail flaps readiness.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Require 3 consecutive fails before going unhealthy:
FAIL_THRESHOLD = 3
async def periodic_check():
    fails = 0
    while True:
        ok = await _check_deps()
        fails = 0 if ok else fails + 1
        STATE['deps_ok'] = fails < FAIL_THRESHOLD
        await asyncio.sleep(15)

production_checklist: Always have BOTH liveness + readiness - the orchestrator needs to distinguish them.
Use startupProbe in K8s for slow model loads - prevents premature liveness kills.
Probe a real inference (small prompt) in startup, not just 'process is up'.
Add downstream dep checks (vLLM, Redis, Postgres) to readiness - probe should fail if any critical dep is down.
Don't fail readiness on FIRST dep failure - use consecutive-fail threshold to avoid flapping.
Pipe /readyz response to a structured logger - mid-flight readiness changes are signal.
Add /metrics endpoint alongside (Prometheus) for the same dependency info.

faq.question: Liveness vs readiness vs startup probe - which when?
faq.answer: Startup: 'has the slow init finished?' - used during pod boot only, disables liveness checks until passed. Readiness: 'should I send traffic?' - used continuously by service discovery. Liveness: 'should I be restarted?' - last resort, fires on process hang. Most LLM bugs need readiness, not liveness.

faq.question: Should /healthz check downstream deps?
faq.answer: No - liveness should be cheap + local. If your DB is down, restarting your pod doesn't help. Put dep checks in /readyz so the orchestrator stops sending traffic but doesn't restart unnecessarily.

title: Modal Serverless GPU LLM Deploy (Llama 3.3 70B)
tldr: Deploy Llama 3.3 70B as a serverless GPU endpoint on Modal with cold-start optimization (model layer caching) and per-request billing.
description: Production Modal app: 4xH100 image, vLLM-backed endpoint, model weights cached on a Modal Volume to cut cold-starts from 8min to 90s.
when_to_use: Sporadic-traffic LLM endpoint (1-1000 QPS bursts) where you want GPU serverless. Modal's per-request billing + scale-to-zero is meaningfully cheaper than an always-on H100 box when traffic is < 30% of full utilization.
when_not_to_use: Skip Modal when (1) sustained high QPS - dedicated infra is cheaper at >50% utilization; (2) you need <100ms TTFT every time - cold starts add 60-90s; (3) you're price-sensitive to overall cost - hosted Groq/Together is cheaper end-to-end.
quick_start: 1) `pip install modal`  2) `modal token new`  3) `modal deploy llama_app.py`  4) Hit the deployed URL with curl.
full_code:
"""Modal serverless deployment of Llama 3.3 70B with vLLM."""
from __future__ import annotations

import modal


MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"
APP = "ossai-llama-server"


# 1. Image: CUDA 12.4 + vLLM 0.7
image = (
    modal.Image.debian_slim(python_version="3.11")
    .pip_install("vllm==0.7.0", "huggingface_hub[hf_transfer]==0.27.0", extra_options="--extra-index-url=https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124")
    .env({"HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER": "1"})
)

# 2. Volume: persist model weights across container starts
vol = modal.Volume.from_name("llama33-weights", create_if_missing=True)

app = modal.App(APP, image=image)


@app.function(
    gpu="H100:4",                      # 4 H100s
    volumes={"/cache": vol},
    secrets=[modal.Secret.from_name("hf-token")],
    timeout=3600,
    keep_warm=1,                       # 1 instance kept warm to cut cold starts further
    allow_concurrent_inputs=64,        # batch inference within instance
)
@modal.web_endpoint(method="POST")
async def generate(payload: dict) -> dict:
    import os
    os.environ["HF_HOME"] = "/cache/hf"

    from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

    llm = _get_or_init_llm()
    sampling = SamplingParams(
        temperature=payload.get("temperature", 0.4),
        max_tokens=payload.get("max_tokens", 500),
    )
    prompts = [payload["prompt"]]
    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling)
    return {"text": outputs[0].outputs[0].text, "model": MODEL}


_LLM: object | None = None


def _get_or_init_llm():
    global _LLM
    if _LLM is None:
        from vllm import LLM
        _LLM = LLM(model=MODEL, tensor_parallel_size=4, gpu_memory_utilization=0.92, max_num_seqs=64)
    return _LLM


# Local entrypoint for testing: `modal run llama_app.py::main --prompt "Hello"`
@app.local_entrypoint()
def main(prompt: str = "Explain Modal in one sentence."):
    print(generate.remote({"prompt": prompt, "max_tokens": 100}))

vol = modal.Volume.from_name('llama33-weights', create_if_missing=True)
app = modal.App('ossai-llama', image=image)

@app.function(gpu='H100:4', volumes={'/cache': vol}, keep_warm=1)
@modal.web_endpoint(method='POST')
async def generate(payload: dict) -> dict:
    from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
    llm = LLM(model='meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct', tensor_parallel_size=4)
    out = llm.generate([payload['prompt']], SamplingParams(max_tokens=500))
    return {'text': out[0].outputs[0].text}

production_checklist: Persist weights to a Modal Volume - re-downloading on every cold start is the #1 cost mistake.
Use keep_warm=1 for predictable latency - one instance always live.
Set scaledown_window=60-300s to avoid thrashing on sporadic traffic.
Pin vLLM + Modal versions in the Image - avoid silent breakage on upgrade.
Bake the HF download into the image when latency is critical (no first-request delay).
Monitor Modal billing dashboard - serverless can surprise on sudden traffic spikes.
Have a Together AI / Groq fallback URL for outage recovery.

faq.question: Modal vs RunPod serverless?
faq.answer: Modal: deep Python integration, great DX, slightly more expensive. RunPod: cheaper GPU rates, container-only (you bring a Dockerfile), less Python-native. For pure cost optimization with bursty workloads, RunPod wins. For developer experience + complex apps, Modal wins.

faq.question: When is Modal cheaper than an always-on H100?
faq.answer: Roughly when utilization is < 30% (i.e. fewer than ~7 hours/day of active inference). Above that, dedicated H100 from Lambda/Crusoe/Coreweave wins on dollars-per-token.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Kubernetes Helm Chart for vLLM (GPU + HPA + PDB)
tldr: Helm chart deploying vLLM with GPU node affinity, HPA on tokens-per-sec, PDB, persistent model cache PVC, and Prometheus ServiceMonitor.
description: Production vLLM K8s deployment: GPU pool node affinity, HPA on custom Prometheus metric (vllm:num_requests_waiting), PDB, model-cache PVC, ServiceMonitor.
when_to_use: You self-host LLMs in K8s and want a reusable Helm chart. The recipe handles GPU node affinity (nvidia.com/gpu.product), PVC for model cache (avoid 140GB redownload on every pod), HPA on real LLM signals (queue depth, not CPU), and PodDisruptionBudget for graceful rolling updates.
when_not_to_use: Skip Helm + K8s when (1) you have <3 GPU nodes - the orchestration overhead isn't worth it; (2) you're early-stage - serverless (Modal/RunPod) is faster to iterate; (3) you don't have K8s expertise on the team.
quick_start: 1) Provision a GPU node pool with nvidia-device-plugin  2) Save the chart files  3) `helm install vllm . -f values.yaml`
full_code:
# values.yaml -- vLLM Helm chart for Llama 3.3 70B
replicaCount: 1
image:
  repository: vllm/vllm-openai
  tag: "v0.7.0"

resources:
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 4
    memory: 256Gi
  requests:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 4
    memory: 128Gi

nodeSelector:
  nvidia.com/gpu.product: NVIDIA-H100-80GB-HBM3

# Persistent model cache (avoid re-downloading 140GB on pod restart)
modelCache:
  enabled: true
  size: 200Gi
  storageClass: gp3   # or fast-ssd, depending on cloud

env:
  - name: HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN
    valueFrom: {secretKeyRef: {name: hf-token, key: token}}
  - name: HF_HOME
    value: /cache/hf

args:
  - "--model"
  - "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"
  - "--tensor-parallel-size"
  - "4"
  - "--max-num-seqs"
  - "256"
  - "--enable-prefix-caching"
  - "--gpu-memory-utilization"
  - "0.92"

service:
  type: ClusterIP
  port: 8000

# HPA based on vLLM's request queue depth (Prometheus custom metric)
autoscaling:
  enabled: true
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 4
  customMetrics:
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric: {name: vllm_num_requests_waiting}
        target: {type: AverageValue, averageValue: "5"}

# PodDisruptionBudget for safe rolling updates
podDisruptionBudget:
  enabled: true
  minAvailable: 1

# Prometheus ServiceMonitor (Prometheus Operator)
serviceMonitor:
  enabled: true
  interval: 15s
  path: /metrics
setup_steps: Provision K8s cluster with GPU node pool (EKS+karpenter, GKE, AKS, or self-managed).
Install Prometheus Operator (for HPA on custom metrics).
Create HF secret: `kubectl create secret generic hf-token --from-literal=token=hf_xxx`
Save values.yaml + Helm chart skeleton (templates/deployment.yaml, hpa.yaml, etc.).
Verify: `kubectl get pods -n llm-system -w` (first pod takes 10+ min to download weights).

production_checklist: Always mount model-cache PVC - 140GB downloads on every restart are expensive + slow.
Use HPA on real LLM signal (queue depth) not CPU - LLM serving is GPU-bound not CPU-bound.
Set PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable=1 (or higher) - K8s rolling updates can otherwise drop all replicas.
Use spot/preemptible GPUs only when paired with PDB + retries - eviction is the norm not exception.
Pin vLLM image tag - latest is moving + occasionally regresses.
Enable startupProbe with 600s threshold - first start downloads weights.
Pre-warm HF cache via a Job that runs before the Deployment - clean cold-start story.

faq.question: Why HPA on queue depth instead of GPU utilization?
faq.answer: GPU util doesn't drop when requests pile up - vLLM batches continuously, so util can be 95% with deep queues. Queue depth (vllm:num_requests_waiting) is the direct latency signal.

faq.question: Spot GPUs - worth the savings?
faq.answer: Yes if you have 3+ replicas + PDB + retry logic. Eviction is frequent (10-30% chance per hour on H100 spot). For 1-2 replicas, on-demand is the right call - eviction = outage.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Docker Multi-Stage LLM Image (Slim, Secure)
tldr: Multi-stage Dockerfile separating builder (PyTorch + CUDA) from runtime (slim Python + .whl artifacts). 60% smaller final image.
description: Two-stage build: stage 1 compiles wheels, stage 2 is a slim CUDA runtime that only contains the wheels + non-root user. Saves ~5GB on a vLLM image.
when_to_use: You're shipping an LLM container to K8s, ECS, or Modal and want fast pull times + minimal attack surface. Multi-stage cuts a typical vLLM image from 18GB to 7GB, drops the build toolchain, and runs as non-root.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you're using a vendor image (vllm/vllm-openai) directly - just pin the tag; (2) build time matters more than image size - single-stage is faster; (3) you're prototyping.
quick_start: 1) Save the Dockerfile  2) `docker build -t ossai/vllm:0.7.0 .`  3) Push to registry.
full_code:
# Multi-stage LLM Docker image: slim + secure + reproducible.
#
# build:   docker build -t ossai/vllm:0.7.0 .
# run dev: docker run --gpus all -p 8000:8000 -v $(pwd)/cache:/cache ossai/vllm:0.7.0

# ============================================================
# Stage 1: Builder - has full toolchain
# ============================================================
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-devel-ubuntu22.04 AS builder

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    python3.11 python3.11-dev python3-pip build-essential git \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.txt .

# Build a wheelhouse so the runtime stage doesn't need a compiler
RUN python3.11 -m pip wheel --wheel-dir /wheels -r requirements.txt

# ============================================================
# Stage 2: Runtime - slim, non-root, no build toolchain
# ============================================================
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04 AS runtime

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1 \
    HF_HOME=/cache/hf

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    python3.11 python3-pip ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* \
    && groupadd -r app && useradd -r -g app -d /app -s /sbin/nologin app \
    && mkdir -p /app /cache && chown -R app:app /app /cache

# Install pre-built wheels from the builder stage
COPY --from=builder /wheels /wheels
RUN python3.11 -m pip install --no-index --find-links=/wheels vllm==0.7.0 \
    && rm -rf /wheels

WORKDIR /app
COPY --chown=app:app serve.py .
USER app

EXPOSE 8000
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD curl -fsS http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

CMD ["python3.11", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", \
     "--model", "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct", \
     "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
code:
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-devel-ubuntu22.04 AS builder
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y python3.11 python3-pip build-essential
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN python3.11 -m pip wheel --wheel-dir /wheels -r requirements.txt

FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y python3.11 python3-pip && useradd -r app
COPY --from=builder /wheels /wheels
RUN pip install --no-index --find-links=/wheels vllm==0.7.0
USER app
CMD ["python3.11", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", "--model", "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"]
setup_steps: Create `requirements.txt` with your pinned deps (vllm==0.7.0, transformers, etc.).
Verify size: `docker images ossai/vllm` - should be ~7GB (vs ~18GB single-stage).
(Optional) Run trivy scan: `trivy image ossai/vllm:0.7.0` - the slim runtime has fewer CVEs.

variations.label: BuildKit cache mounts for faster rebuilds
variations.description: Cache pip + apt across builds.
variations.code_snippet:
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y python3.11 \
    && python3.11 -m pip install vllm==0.7.0

variations.label: Distroless final stage
variations.description: Even smaller, even more secure.
variations.code_snippet:
FROM gcr.io/distroless/python3-debian12 AS runtime
COPY --from=builder /wheels /wheels
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11 /usr/local/lib/python3.11
USER nonroot:nonroot
CMD ["-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server"]

common_errors.cause: Used `-devel` instead of `-runtime` CUDA in stage 2.
common_errors.fix_snippet: FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04   # NOT -devel

common_errors.error_text: Build fails: 'No matching distribution found for vllm'
common_errors.cause: Wheelhouse build failed silently in stage 1.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Debug stage 1 alone:
docker build --target builder -t test .
docker run --rm test ls /wheels  # check wheels exist

common_errors.error_text: Container fails at runtime: 'cannot open shared library cuDNN'
common_errors.cause: Runtime image doesn't include cuDNN that the dev image had.
common_errors.fix_snippet:
# Install cuDNN explicitly in runtime stage:
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libcudnn8 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

production_checklist: Always use `:runtime` base in the final stage, never `:devel`.
Run as non-root - the LLM doesn't need root and it's a CVE-mitigation no-brainer.
Pin EVERY base image tag - `:latest` will break you eventually.
Use BuildKit cache mounts in CI - 5-10x faster rebuilds.
Trivy-scan the image in CI - LLM stacks have many transitive deps + occasional CVEs.
Set HEALTHCHECK so orchestrators detect bad pods.
Multi-arch build (amd64 + arm64) via docker buildx for mac dev parity.

faq.question: Should I use the official vllm/vllm-openai image instead?
faq.answer: For most cases yes - it's pinned, slim, maintained. Roll your own when (a) you need additional deps in the same image (your own server code, monitoring agents), (b) you want distroless for security, (c) you're stacking custom kernels.

faq.question: Why not just bake the model weights in?
faq.answer: 70B is 140GB - far too big for an image. PVC/Volume at runtime is the right pattern. Bake weights for small models (<10GB) where image pull beats download.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: RunPod Serverless vLLM Endpoint (Scale-to-Zero)
tldr: RunPod serverless container running vLLM. Scale-to-zero, ~30s cold start, pay-per-second GPU. Cheaper than always-on for sporadic traffic.
description: RunPod serverless template: vLLM container + handler.py + Dockerfile. Per-second GPU billing; 30-60s cold start; scales to zero when idle.
when_to_use: Sporadic LLM traffic (eval pipelines, internal tools, demos). RunPod serverless GPUs bill per-second, scale to zero at idle, and routinely cost 30-60% less than Modal for the same workload. Best fit when you don't need <500ms cold-start latency.
when_not_to_use: Skip RunPod when (1) you need <30s cold start - Modal's keep_warm is better; (2) you need a vendor-managed model registry; (3) you're not comfortable writing Dockerfiles.
quick_start: 1) Write a handler.py + Dockerfile  2) Push to a container registry  3) Create a Serverless endpoint in the RunPod console  4) Call via the RunPod API.
full_code:
# Dockerfile -- RunPod serverless vLLM template
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    HF_HOME=/runpod-volume/hf

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    python3.11 python3-pip ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN python3.11 -m pip install --no-cache-dir \
    runpod>=1.7.0 \
    vllm==0.7.0

WORKDIR /app
COPY handler.py .

CMD ["python3.11", "-u", "handler.py"]

# ============================================================
# handler.py -- Save this alongside the Dockerfile
# ============================================================
# import os
# import runpod
# from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
#
# llm = LLM(
#     model=os.environ.get("MODEL", "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"),
#     gpu_memory_utilization=0.92,
#     enable_prefix_caching=True,
#     max_num_seqs=int(os.environ.get("MAX_SEQS", "64")),
# )
#
# def handler(event):
#     payload = event.get("input", {})
#     prompts = payload.get("prompts") or [payload.get("prompt", "")]
#     params = SamplingParams(
#         temperature=payload.get("temperature", 0.4),
#         max_tokens=payload.get("max_tokens", 500),
#     )
#     outputs = llm.generate(prompts, params)
#     return [{"text": o.outputs[0].text, "tokens_out": len(o.outputs[0].token_ids)} for o in outputs]
#
# runpod.serverless.start({"handler": handler})

# Dockerfile
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y python3.11 python3-pip
RUN pip install runpod>=1.7.0 vllm==0.7.0
COPY handler.py .
CMD ["python3.11", "-u", "handler.py"]
setup_steps: Sign up at runpod.io and add credit.
Push to a registry: `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t your-registry/vllm-serverless:1 --push .`
In RunPod console: Serverless -> New Endpoint -> Custom container -> point at your image.
Attach a Network Volume for HF cache (mount at /runpod-volume).
Configure: workers 0-3, GPU type (A100 80GB), idle timeout 5s, max execution time 600s.
Test via the RunPod API: `curl -X POST https://api.runpod.ai/v2/<endpoint_id>/runsync -H 'Authorization: Bearer ...' -d '{"input": {"prompt": "hi"}}'`

production_checklist: Attach Network Volume + HF_HOME=/runpod-volume/hf - avoids the 140GB redownload.
Build images for linux/amd64 explicitly - apple silicon builds will fail at runtime.
Set idle timeout 5-30s - longer = warmer cache, higher cost.
Use the RunPod OpenAI-compatible vLLM template if you don't need custom handler code.
Pin vLLM + runpod versions in the Dockerfile.
Test cold start latency from your actual region - east/west pricing + latency differs.
Monitor the RunPod billing page - serverless costs can balloon with bad cold-start patterns.

faq.question: RunPod vs Modal vs Lambda Labs?
faq.answer: RunPod: cheapest serverless GPU per second, container-only DX. Modal: best Python DX, slightly pricier. Lambda Labs: best for always-on instances, no serverless. For cost-sensitive bursty workloads RunPod wins; for developer experience Modal does.

faq.question: Network volume vs baking weights in image?
faq.answer: Network volume: pulls weights once, shared across workers, cheaper. Baking weights in image: faster cold start (no download), bigger image pull. For 8B models bake them in; for 70B+ use volumes.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Cloudflare Workers AI Edge Deploy (Llama 3.1 8B Global)
tldr: Cloudflare Workers AI deploy of Llama 3.1 8B at the edge. Global latency <100ms. Free-tier limits + paid-tier billing config.
description: TypeScript Worker that runs Llama 3.1 8B via the Workers AI binding. Edge-replicated globally; streams via SSE; per-request billing.
when_to_use: Global apps that need sub-100ms TTFT for users worldwide. Cloudflare Workers AI runs models at every PoP - your Tokyo users hit Tokyo, your Berlin users hit Berlin. Great for chat widgets, side-projects, and demos. Free tier covers ~10k requests/day.
when_not_to_use: Skip Workers AI when (1) you need Llama 3.3 70B - WAI runs smaller models (8B-13B); (2) you need fine-tuned weights - WAI is base models only; (3) sustained high QPS - paid tier rates beat hosted Groq above ~100k req/day.
quick_start: 1) `npm create cloudflare@latest my-worker -- --type=hello-world`  2) Add `[ai] binding='AI'` to wrangler.toml  3) `npx wrangler deploy`

env_vars.name: CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN
env_vars.description: From dash.cloudflare.com -> My Profile -> API Tokens. Workers AI scope.

setup_steps: Sign up for a Cloudflare account (free).
Test locally: `npx wrangler dev` and curl localhost:8787/chat.
Deploy: `npx wrangler deploy` - rolls to all Cloudflare PoPs globally.
Test global latency: from different regions, hit https://your-worker.workers.dev/chat.

production_checklist: Use AI Gateway in front for caching, observability, rate limits - free.
Always set max_tokens - unbounded outputs eat the neuron budget.
Pin compatibility_date in wrangler.toml - prevents silent API breakage.
Use the `@cf/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct` ID format - generic model names aren't supported.
For high-traffic: Workers Paid ($5/mo) + AI Gateway. Caching alone often hits 50-70% hit rate on FAQ chat.
Test global latency from your top-3 regions before claiming 'global' performance.
Don't run sensitive prompts through WAI - Cloudflare sees them; use your own VPC for that.

faq.question: Workers AI vs Anthropic / OpenAI API?
faq.answer: Workers AI: edge-replicated (low latency globally), one-binding setup, smaller models only (8B-13B). Anthropic/OpenAI: bigger models, US-centric latency, paid-from-call-1. For chat widgets + global apps with simple needs, Workers AI is hard to beat. For frontier capability, hosted APIs.

faq.question: Can I use my own fine-tuned model?
faq.answer: Not directly on Workers AI. Workarounds: (a) host the fine-tune on Hugging Face Endpoints / Replicate and fetch() from the Worker, (b) use AI Gateway as a proxy to your own endpoint, (c) wait - Cloudflare keeps adding model support.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: HuggingFace TRL SFTTrainer (Vanilla Single-File Recipe)
tldr: Vanilla SFT with TRL SFTTrainer: packed sequences, chat template, FlashAttention 2, gradient checkpointing. Single-file recipe.
description: Baseline SFT recipe everyone should start from. No magic - just TRL's SFTTrainer wired correctly with the right chat template, packing, and gradient checkpointing.
when_to_use: Your first fine-tune. TRL SFTTrainer is the no-frills baseline - you understand exactly what's happening, and other recipes (Unsloth, Axolotl) make sense as optimizations on top of this.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need 2x speedup - use Unsloth; (2) you need multi-GPU - use Axolotl; (3) you need GUI - use LLaMA-Factory.
quick_start: 1) `pip install trl transformers datasets peft`  2) Prepare HF dataset  3) `python sft.py`
full_code:
"""Vanilla TRL SFTTrainer with sane defaults."""
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig
from peft import LoraConfig


MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.pad_token or tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", attn_implementation="flash_attention_2")

ds = load_dataset("yahma/alpaca-cleaned", split="train[:5000]")

def format_chat(ex):
    msgs = [{"role": "user", "content": ex["instruction"]}, {"role": "assistant", "content": ex["output"]}]
    return {"text": tokenizer.apply_chat_template(msgs, tokenize=False)}

ds = ds.map(format_chat)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=ds,
    peft_config=LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"]),
    args=SFTConfig(
        output_dir="./out",
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
        num_train_epochs=2,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        bf16=True,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        packing=True,
        max_seq_length=4096,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=500,
        optim="adamw_torch",
    ),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/final")

production_checklist: Always set the chat template before training - mismatched templates wreck the model.
Use packing=True - 30% faster training on short examples.
Eval on a held-out set every 500 steps - catches overfitting early.
Pin TRL version (0.13 vs 0.14 changes SFTConfig fields subtly).
Start with LoRA r=16; only bump after measuring need.
Save_total_limit=2 to avoid disk filling on long runs.
Test inference with the saved adapter BEFORE merging - catches save/load bugs.

faq.question: TRL SFTTrainer vs Unsloth?
faq.answer: TRL: baseline, no magic, easy to debug. Unsloth: 2x faster + 30% less VRAM via custom kernels. Use TRL when learning fine-tuning; switch to Unsloth once you have a working baseline.

faq.question: Why packing=True?
faq.answer: Concatenates short examples into max_seq_length chunks - dramatically improves GPU utilization on datasets with mixed-length examples. ~30% faster training, no quality cost when implemented correctly.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: Continued Pretraining for Domain Adaptation (Medical/Legal/Code)
tldr: Continued pretraining of Llama 3.1 8B on a domain corpus (medical, legal, code) before instruction-tuning. Avoids catastrophic forgetting.
description: Two-stage adaptation: (1) CPT on raw domain text with very low LR + LoRA on attention only; (2) SFT on domain instructions afterward.
when_to_use: Your domain has vocabulary the base model wasn't trained on (medical codes, legal citations, proprietary code). CPT on raw domain text exposes the model to domain language before instruction fine-tuning - typically 5-15% lift on domain QA tasks.
when_not_to_use: Skip CPT when (1) general SFT already works; (2) you have <100k tokens of domain text - not enough signal; (3) domain isn't really novel (web data already covers it).
quick_start: 1) Collect a domain corpus as raw text  2) Run CPT with very low LR  3) SFT on domain instructions afterward.
full_code:
"""Continued pretraining (CPT) of Llama 3.1 8B on domain corpus."""
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorForLanguageModeling
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model


MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B"  # BASE, not Instruct - we'll instruct-tune after CPT

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", attn_implementation="flash_attention_2")

# LoRA on attention only - preserves base capability better than full attention+MLP
model = get_peft_model(model, LoraConfig(r=64, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], task_type="CAUSAL_LM"))

# Domain corpus - raw text in 'text' column
ds = load_dataset("json", data_files="medical_corpus.jsonl", split="train")
def tokenize(ex):
    return tokenizer(ex["text"], truncation=True, max_length=4096, return_overflowing_tokens=False)
ds = ds.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=ds.column_names)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=ds,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False),
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./out/cpt",
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=5e-5,        # 10x lower than SFT - preserves base capabilities
        weight_decay=0.01,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        bf16=True,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=1000,
    ),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/cpt/final")
# Then run SFT on domain instructions starting from this checkpoint.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments, DataCollatorForLanguageModeling
from datasets import load_dataset
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B')
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B', torch_dtype='bfloat16')
model = get_peft_model(model, LoraConfig(r=64, target_modules=['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj']))

ds = load_dataset('json', data_files='domain.jsonl', split='train')
ds = ds.map(lambda e: tok(e['text'], truncation=True, max_length=4096), batched=True)

Trainer(model=model, tokenizer=tok, train_dataset=ds,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tok, mlm=False),
    args=TrainingArguments(output_dir='./out', learning_rate=5e-5, num_train_epochs=1, bf16=True)).train()

Start from BASE model (Llama-3.1-8B), not Instruct - we'll instruct after.
Use 10x lower LR than SFT (5e-5 vs 2e-4).
Run 1 epoch only - more causes catastrophic forgetting.
Follow with SFT on domain instructions from the CPT checkpoint.
Eval on domain QA + general benchmarks (MMLU) to verify no forgetting.

production_checklist: Start from BASE not Instruct for CPT.

faq.question: CPT vs RAG for domain knowledge?
faq.answer: RAG: cheaper, dynamic, easy to update - prefer it. CPT: better for domain *language* (vocabulary, structure) but expensive + static. The right answer is usually 'do RAG; consider CPT only if specific knowledge is missing AND your domain has unique vocabulary the model struggles with'.

faq.question: How much corpus do I need?
faq.answer: Minimum useful: 1M tokens of high-quality domain text. Sweet spot: 50-500M tokens. Above 1B tokens you're essentially doing pretraining.

title: Synthetic Instruction Data with Magpie (Self-Instruct via BOS)
tldr: Generate high-quality instruction-tuning data via Magpie: prompt a base model with its own BOS, collect diverse instructions, filter.
description: Magpie technique: feed Llama 3 Instruct only its BOS + chat-start tokens, let it generate the user turn first, then complete with assistant. Highly diverse synthetic SFT data.
when_to_use: You need an instruction-tuning dataset but don't have one for your domain. Magpie is the highest-quality OSS synthetic data generator in 2026 - the data is more diverse than Alpaca + better than self-instruct + free (your own GPU).
when_not_to_use: Skip Magpie when (1) you have real labeled data already - it beats synthetic; (2) you need very narrow domain coverage - human curation will be tighter; (3) you're targeting safety alignment - synthetic alignment data has well-known issues.
quick_start: 1) `pip install vllm transformers`  2) Run Magpie on Llama 3 70B Instruct  3) Filter by quality + dedupe.
full_code:
"""Magpie synthetic instruction data generation via vLLM."""
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
import json, hashlib, random

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"
llm = LLM(model=MODEL, tensor_parallel_size=4, gpu_memory_utilization=0.92)

# Llama 3 chat tokens
USER_START = "<|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>\n\n"
USER_END = "<|eot_id|>"
ASST_START = "<|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>\n\n"

# Stage 1: generate diverse user instructions by feeding ONLY the user header
N = 10000
inst_params = SamplingParams(temperature=1.0, top_p=0.95, max_tokens=512, stop=[USER_END, ASST_START])
instructions = []
for batch in range(0, N, 500):
    prompts = [f"<|begin_of_text|>{USER_START}"] * 500
    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, inst_params)
    instructions.extend([o.outputs[0].text.strip() for o in outputs if o.outputs[0].text.strip()])

# Dedupe by hash
seen = set(); deduped = []
for inst in instructions:
    h = hashlib.md5(inst.encode()).hexdigest()
    if h not in seen and 20 < len(inst) < 500:
        seen.add(h); deduped.append(inst)

# Stage 2: complete each with an assistant response
resp_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=2048, stop=[USER_END])
prompts = [f"<|begin_of_text|>{USER_START}{i}{USER_END}{ASST_START}" for i in deduped]
outputs = llm.generate(prompts, resp_params)

with open("magpie_sft.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for inst, out in zip(deduped, outputs):
        f.write(json.dumps({"instruction": inst, "output": out.outputs[0].text.strip()}) + "\n")
print(f"wrote {len(deduped)} examples to magpie_sft.jsonl")

EOT = '<|eot_id|>'

production_checklist: Run on Instruct, not base - Magpie needs the instruction-following primitive.
Use the largest Instruct model you can (70B beats 8B for diversity + quality).
ALWAYS dedupe by hash - 10-30% of generations repeat.
Filter by length: 20-500 chars instruction, 50-2000 chars response.
Quality-filter with a reward model or LLM judge for the final 10-30%.
Mix Magpie data 50/50 with curated human examples for best results.
Verify diversity - cluster the data and check cluster sizes; small clusters mean over-collapse.

faq.question: Magpie vs self-instruct / Alpaca approach?
faq.answer: Self-instruct (Alpaca): seed with hand-written examples, generate variations. Magpie: no seed needed - the BOS trick produces more diverse data with fewer prompt-engineering pitfalls. Magpie data scores higher on diversity benchmarks + downstream SFT quality.

faq.question: How many Magpie examples do I need?
faq.answer: 10k is the entry point. 100k+ approaches commercial dataset quality. The compute cost is real - 100k examples on 4xA100 is ~12 hours = ~$30 at 70B.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: KTO Fine-Tune (Binary Thumbs-Up/Down Feedback)
tldr: KTO alignment using binary labels (good/bad) instead of paired preferences. Easier data collection than DPO.
description: Kahneman-Tversky Optimization: align with just {prompt, completion, label} instead of {prompt, chosen, rejected}. Works when your feedback is thumbs-up/down not pairs.
when_to_use: You collect thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback from users (chat apps, recommender systems). KTO uses this signal directly - no need to construct chosen/rejected pairs. Drastically simplifies the data collection pipeline.
when_not_to_use: Skip KTO when (1) you have paired preferences - DPO is simpler; (2) feedback is very imbalanced (95% positive) - KTO struggles; (3) you can collect Likert (1-5) - use IPO or direct reward modeling instead.
quick_start: 1) `pip install trl>=0.13`  2) Dataset with {prompt, completion, label: True/False}  3) `python kto.py`
full_code:
"""KTO fine-tune on binary feedback."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
from trl import KTOConfig, KTOTrainer
from peft import LoraConfig
from datasets import load_dataset


MODEL = "chadcorp/llama3_1_8b_ossai_sft_v1"

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tok.pad_token = tok.pad_token or tok.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", device_map="auto")

# Dataset columns: prompt, completion, label (bool: True=thumbs-up, False=thumbs-down)
ds = load_dataset("argilla/ultrafeedback-kto-cleaned", split="train[:10000]")

trainer = KTOTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=KTOConfig(
        output_dir="./out/kto",
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=2,
        num_train_epochs=1,
        learning_rate=5e-6,
        beta=0.1,
        max_length=2048,
        max_prompt_length=1024,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        bf16=True,
        # KTO-specific: importance weights to balance positive vs negative samples
        desirable_weight=1.0,
        undesirable_weight=1.0,
    ),
    tokenizer=tok,
    train_dataset=ds,
    peft_config=LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"]),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/kto/final")

production_checklist: Balance your data - 30-70% positives is healthy; otherwise use weights.
Start from SFT model, NOT base - KTO assumes instruction-following exists.
beta=0.1 default; 0.05 conservative, 0.3 aggressive.
Always compare against SFT-baseline on eval set before shipping.
Pin trl version - KTO API has been moving.
Use small batches + grad accum - KTO can be unstable at large effective batch sizes.
Sample from a held-out feedback set for the eval - don't reuse training data.

faq.question: KTO vs DPO when I have both kinds of data?
faq.answer: If you have paired preferences AND binary feedback: combine them. Convert paired into binary (chosen=True, rejected=False) and feed both to KTOTrainer. You get more data for the same alignment effect.

faq.question: Is KTO better than DPO?
faq.answer: On paired data, DPO and KTO are roughly equivalent. KTO's edge is enabling alignment on data you couldn't otherwise use (binary feedback from production). Use whichever matches your data shape.

attribution: OSS AI Hub - original work, MIT-licensed.
title: GaLore Full Fine-Tune on a 24GB GPU (7B Model)
tldr: GaLore gradient-projection trick: full-parameter fine-tune of 7B models on consumer 24GB GPUs. Better quality than LoRA.
description: GaLore projects gradients onto a low-rank subspace, slashing optimizer memory. Lets you do full-parameter fine-tuning on a 24GB RTX 4090 - LoRA-level VRAM, full-parameter quality.
when_to_use: You want full-parameter fine-tune quality on a consumer GPU (no LoRA bottleneck) without paying for cloud H100s. GaLore on a 4090 fits a full 7B fine-tune in ~22GB VRAM - same memory as a LoRA-r64 run but ~3-5% better on most evals.
when_not_to_use: Skip GaLore when (1) LoRA results are already great - simpler; (2) you need to fine-tune 70B - GaLore alone doesn't scale that high; (3) you need 2x training speed - Unsloth+LoRA is faster.
quick_start: 1) `pip install transformers galore-torch`  2) Replace optimizer with GaLoreAdamW  3) Run with full-param fine-tune.
full_code:
"""GaLore full-param fine-tune of Llama 3 7B on a 24GB GPU."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from datasets import load_dataset
from galore_torch import GaLoreAdamW

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16", attn_implementation="flash_attention_2")

ds = load_dataset("yahma/alpaca-cleaned", split="train[:5000]")
def fmt(ex):
    return {"text": tok.apply_chat_template([{"role":"user","content":ex["instruction"]},{"role":"assistant","content":ex["output"]}], tokenize=False)}
ds = ds.map(fmt)
ds = ds.map(lambda e: tok(e["text"], truncation=True, max_length=2048), batched=True)


# Split params: GaLore-projected (large matrices) vs regular (everything else)
galore_params = []; regular_params = []
target_modules = ["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"]
for n, p in model.named_parameters():
    if any(t in n for t in target_modules):
        galore_params.append(p)
    else:
        regular_params.append(p)


param_groups = [
    {"params": regular_params},
    {"params": galore_params, "rank": 256, "update_proj_gap": 200, "scale": 0.25, "proj_type": "std"},
]
optimizer = GaLoreAdamW(param_groups, lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.01)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tok,
    train_dataset=ds,
    optimizers=(optimizer, None),
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./out/galore",
        per_device_train_batch_size=1,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=16,
        num_train_epochs=2,
        bf16=True,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_steps=500,
    ),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./out/galore/final")

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from galore_torch import GaLoreAdamW

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct', torch_dtype='bfloat16')
galore_params = [p for n,p in model.named_parameters() if any(t in n for t in ['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj'])]
regular_params = [p for n,p in model.named_parameters() if p not in galore_params]

opt = GaLoreAdamW([
    {'params': regular_params},
    {'params': galore_params, 'rank': 256, 'update_proj_gap': 200, 'scale': 0.25, 'proj_type': 'std'},
], lr=2e-5)

production_checklist: Use rank=256 as the starting point - drop to 128 for tight VRAM, raise to 512 for quality.
Use 10x lower LR than LoRA - GaLore is sensitive.
Pair with FlashAttention 2 + gradient checkpointing for max VRAM savings.
Eval against LoRA + full-param baselines - GaLore wins on some tasks, ties on others.
GaLore's main appeal is VRAM, not speed - expect 1.5-3x slower per step vs LoRA.
Save checkpoints every 200 steps - the long runs lose hours on crashes.
Pin galore-torch version - the project is young.

faq.question: GaLore vs LoRA vs QLoRA - which?
faq.answer: LoRA: simplest, fastest, slight quality penalty vs full-param. QLoRA: LoRA + 4-bit base, even less VRAM, marginally lower quality. GaLore: full-param quality at LoRA-ish VRAM, ~2x slower than LoRA. Use GaLore when you measurably need full-param quality and don't have H100 budget.

faq.question: Why isn't everyone using GaLore?
faq.answer: Three reasons: (1) it's 2-3x slower than LoRA, (2) the optimizer + projection introduce hyperparameter complexity (rank, gap, scale, proj_type), (3) LoRA results are 'good enough' for most tasks. GaLore shines on hard tasks where 3-5% matters.

title: Arena-Hard ELO Benchmark Runner (Compare to LMSys Arena)
tldr: Run Arena-Hard-Auto eval against your model, get an ELO rating comparable to LMSys Arena. Useful for model selection.
description: Arena-Hard-Auto is a 500-prompt benchmark designed to correlate with LMSys Chatbot Arena ELO. Run it on your fine-tune to compare to GPT-5/Claude/Llama scores.
when_to_use: You fine-tuned a chat model and want to know where it lands relative to public models on chat quality. Arena-Hard's 500 hard prompts + auto-judge produces an ELO that correlates ~0.98 with LMSys Arena - much cheaper than human evals.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you have a narrow domain - Arena-Hard prompts are general; (2) you need per-task metrics - Arena-Hard outputs one number; (3) cost-sensitive - judging costs $5-20 per run.
quick_start: 1) `git clone https://github.com/lmarena/arena-hard-auto`  2) Generate completions  3) Judge with stronger model  4) Compute ELO.
full_code:
"""Arena-Hard-Auto runner against your endpoint."""
import json, subprocess
from pathlib import Path


# 1. Generate model answers
def gen_model_answers(model_id: str, endpoint: str, out: Path):
    """For each Arena-Hard prompt, query your model and save answer."""
    prompts = json.loads(Path("data/arena-hard-v0.1/question.jsonl").read_text())
    answers = []
    for p in prompts:
        # call your model
        import httpx
        r = httpx.post(endpoint, json={"model": model_id, "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": p["turns"][0]["content"]}], "max_tokens": 4096}, timeout=120)
        answers.append({"question_id": p["question_id"], "answer_id": f"{model_id}_{p['question_id']}", "model_id": model_id, "choices": [{"index": 0, "turns": [{"content": r.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]}]}]})
    Path(out).write_text("\n".join(json.dumps(a) for a in answers))


# 2. Judge with stronger model (the Arena-Hard repo provides gen_judgment.py)
# python gen_judgment.py --model your_model --baseline gpt-4-0314 --judge gpt-4-1106-preview
def run_judge(model_id: str, baseline: str = "gpt-4-0314", judge: str = "gpt-5"):
    subprocess.run(["python", "gen_judgment.py", "--model", model_id, "--baseline", baseline, "--judge", judge], check=True)


# 3. Compute ELO
# python show_result.py
def show_results():
    subprocess.run(["python", "show_result.py"], check=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    gen_model_answers("ossai-llama-v1", "http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions", Path("data/arena-hard-v0.1/model_answer/ossai-llama-v1.jsonl"))
    run_judge("ossai-llama-v1")
    show_results()

production_checklist: Always use temperature=0 on both model and judge for reproducibility.
Pin the judge model version - 'gpt-5' moves; 'gpt-5-2026-05-01' doesn't.

faq.question: Arena-Hard vs MMLU?
faq.answer: MMLU: knowledge benchmark, 4-choice multi-choice, deterministic eval. Arena-Hard: open-ended chat quality, LLM-judged, correlates with user preference. Both have value - MMLU for capability, Arena-Hard for preference.

faq.question: How does Arena-Hard ELO map to LMSys Arena ELO?
faq.answer: They correlate ~0.98 (per the Arena-Hard paper). Numerically close but not identical - Arena-Hard tends to be ~50-100 ELO lower than LMSys for the same model. Use it as a leading indicator.

title: HumanEval / HumanEval+ / MBPP Code Benchmark Runner
tldr: Run HumanEval / HumanEval+ / MBPP on any code LLM. Docker sandbox for safe code execution + pass@k computation.
description: bigcode-evaluation-harness pattern: load benchmark, generate completions, execute in sandbox, compute pass@1, pass@10, pass@100.
when_to_use: You're evaluating a code model (Llama 3.1, Qwen2.5-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder, your fine-tune). HumanEval is the standard - 164 hand-written Python problems with hidden tests; HumanEval+ has 80x more tests per problem.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you need long-context code eval (CodeAct/SWE-bench better); (2) non-Python code (use MultiPL-E); (3) novel benchmark needed - HumanEval is leaked into training corpora.
quick_start: 1) `pip install bigcode-evaluation-harness`  2) Docker running for sandbox  3) `bigcode-eval --tasks humanevalplus --model your-model`
full_code:
"""HumanEval+ runner with Docker sandbox via bigcode-evaluation-harness."""
import subprocess

# 1. Generate completions (uses vLLM as backend for speed)
subprocess.run([
    "python", "-m", "bigcode_eval.main",
    "--model", "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    "--tasks", "humanevalplus,mbpp",
    "--max_length_generation", "1024",
    "--temperature", "0.2",
    "--n_samples", "10",            # for pass@10
    "--allow_code_execution",        # required for actual code eval
    "--use_auth_token",
    "--save_generations",
    "--save_generations_path", "./generations.jsonl",
    "--metric_output_path", "./metrics.json",
], check=True)

# Read results
import json
metrics = json.loads(open("./metrics.json").read())
for task, results in metrics.items():
    if "pass@1" in results:
        print(f"{task}: pass@1={results['pass@1']:.3f}  pass@10={results.get('pass@10', 'N/A')}")

faq.question: HumanEval vs MBPP vs LiveCodeBench?
faq.answer: HumanEval: 164 problems, Python only, the standard. MBPP: 974 problems, lower difficulty, more contamination. LiveCodeBench: monthly new problems from LeetCode, lowest contamination but smallest. Run all three for a complete picture.

faq.question: Why are HumanEval+ scores lower than HumanEval?
faq.answer: HumanEval+ adds 80x more test cases per problem. Many models pass the canonical tests but fail edge cases. HumanEval+ is what you should trust.

title: SWE-bench Verified Agent Runner
tldr: Run a coding agent against SWE-bench Verified (500 real GitHub issues). Docker sandbox, log capture, pass@1 scoring.
description: SWE-bench Verified is the gold standard for code-agent eval. Real GitHub issues, real PRs as ground truth, real test suites that must pass.
when_to_use: You're building a coding agent (Claude Code / Cursor / Devin-shaped). SWE-bench Verified is the most credible eval - if your agent scores well there, it's actually fixing real bugs.
when_not_to_use: Skip when (1) you only need code-completion eval - HumanEval is faster; (2) you can't afford ~$50-200 in API costs per run; (3) you don't have a multi-turn agent.
quick_start: 1) Clone the SWE-bench repo  2) Build Docker images for each repo  3) Run your agent on each instance  4) Submit predictions for scoring.
full_code:
"""SWE-bench Verified runner skeleton."""
import json
from datasets import load_dataset
from your_agent import solve_issue   # your coding agent

dataset = load_dataset("princeton-nlp/SWE-bench_Verified", split="test")

predictions = []
for instance in dataset:
    repo = instance["repo"]
    issue = instance["problem_statement"]
    base_commit = instance["base_commit"]
    instance_id = instance["instance_id"]

    # 1. Clone repo + checkout base commit (your agent's environment setup)
    # 2. Feed agent: issue text + repo files (your agent decides what to read)
    # 3. Agent emits a diff (patch) to apply
    patch = solve_issue(issue=issue, repo_path=f"/repos/{repo}", base_commit=base_commit)

    predictions.append({"instance_id": instance_id, "model_name_or_path": "ossai-agent-v1", "model_patch": patch})

with open("predictions.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for p in predictions:
        f.write(json.dumps(p) + "\n")

# Then run the official evaluator (separate process):
# python -m swebench.harness.run_evaluation \
#   --dataset_name princeton-nlp/SWE-bench_Verified \
#   --predictions_path predictions.jsonl \
#   --max_workers 4 \
#   --run_id ossai-v1
